content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

AIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1 O K Street
m onto 95814
322-5660

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

December 16, 1975

Robert J. Nicholas
19449 Riverside Dr.
P.O. Box Y
Sonoma, Ca. 95476

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-210

Dear Mr. Nicholas:

Thank you for your letter of November 25, 1975, requesting an opinion with respect to your obligations under the Political Reform Act. Since your request does not raise a novel question involving interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. I hope, however, that the following comments will be helpful.

It is the informal view of the staff that your gifts of smoked turkeys need not be reported by you so long as you engage in no other activities which constitute attempts to influence state legislative or administrative action. Government Code Section 86108(b) generally requires that a person must report all payments made for the purpose of influencing state legislative or administrative action if such payments exceed two hundred fifty dollars in a month. However, this reporting requirement does not apply if all such payments directly or indirectly benefit elective state officials, legislative officials, agency officials or members of the immediate family of any such official as described in Government Code Section 82045(c). The facts set forth in your letter indicate that all payments made by you which may constitute payments to influence state legislative or administrative action were made in the form of smoked turkeys given to such officials. On the basis of these facts, you may continue to make gifts of smoked turkeys to these officials without incurring any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321, a copy of which is attached hereto. If you have any further questions, please contact Ted Prim, an attorney with the Commission staff.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the Commission

DBL;pvp
Enclosure

Nicholas Turkey Breeding Farms, Inc.

November 25, 1975

CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K Building
K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

This is a request for an opinion. Our company is a primary
breeder of turkeys. That is, we sell the parents of market turkeys.
We are based out of Sonoma, California. For about the past ten years,
our company has said thank you to its suppliers and friends at the
holiday season by presenting smoked turkeys. Enclosed is a copy of
our last year's presentation. These turkeys average seven to eight
pounds in weight which, based upon the current prices at Safeway,
would mean they would be worth between $8.33 and $9.52. We have
quite a bit of pride in them and believe that they are unique.

Our problem is that, with the growing complexity of society,
we have found people in Sacramento (both in the Legislature and
in the agencies) who have assisted us in dealing with these complexities. May we continue to give these turkeys? If so, what reporting
requirements need we and the recipients follow?

We would appreciate hearing from you as soon as possible because
our presentation certificates have already been printed and the turkeys
will be killed early next week. I apologize for the shortness of the
delay, but I have only just realized there might be a problem.

Thanking you for any assistance you may be able to give us and
wishing you the happiest of holidays, I remain with best wishes.

Very truly yours,

NICHOLAS TURKEY BREEDING FARMS, INC.

Robert J. Nicholas
President

RJN:plh
Enclosure

12149 RIVERSIDE DRIVE • P.O. BOXY • SONOMA, CALIFORNIA 95476 • TEL. (707) 996-3561 • TELEX 510 745 9257
Nicholas Breeding Centers: Sonoma, California • Petaluma, California • Dillon Beach, California
Santa Rosa, California • Cannon Falls, Minnesota

ONE IN A MILLION

For many years, Nicholas Turkey Breeding Farms has shown its
appreciation to its friends by presenting smoked turkeys during the holiday
season. The first recipients were on the staff at the Bank of America.
They were selected because that institution has supported, for over forty
years, what we have been trying to achieve. Since that beginning, more
and more cherished relationships have come into being, so we have
given more and more turkeys. In 1973 we said "thank you" with
ninety-three smoked turkeys.

1974 has been a disastrous year for the turkey industry. The spread
between cost of production and market price received has varied between
three and fourteen cents a pound. Some companies in the U.S. raised
over 200 million pounds this year. We made a profit. Not as much as we
had projected, but low enough so that we are eliminating unnecessary
costs. Our smoked turkey program is not being eliminated
—we are expanding it! After all, when times are hard, isn't that when
you appreciate support the most?

In 1974 we are giving one of these turkeys for each million turkeys
produced in the U.S. There were 133 million turkeys produced, so we are
presenting 133 of these turkeys—"one in a million."

We have 27 pure and synthetic blood lines of turkeys on our farms.
Five of these lines produce the genetic material we sell. Twenty lines will
be maintained to try to improve what we sell. Two of the present lines
will join the more than one hundred which have been discarded.

This smoked turkey comes from the line which has more meat and less
bone than any turkey in the world. We maintain a few hundred pedigreed
hens to improve the breed. Some of the offspring are used to replace the
pedigree population, most are sold at sixteen weeks, the rest are smoked.
We hand selected the best non-pedigree turkeys, raised them for an
additional ten weeks for more flavor, smoked them, and selected the
133 best for this presentation.

They truly are "one in a million"—so are you.

NICHOLAS TURKEY BREEDING FARMS, INC.

sections.question: May we continue to give these turkeys? If so, what reporting requirements need we and the recipients follow?
sections.conclusion: It is the informal view of the staff that your gifts of smoked turkeys need not be reported by you so long as you engage in no other activities which constitute attempts to influence state legislative or administrative action. On the basis of these facts, you may continue to make gifts of smoked turkeys to these officials without incurring any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a turkey breeding company can gift smoked turkeys to state legislative and administrative officials and what reporting requirements apply under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concluded that gifts of smoked turkeys to state officials do not require reporting under Government Code Section 86108(b) because the gifts directly benefit elective state officials and legislative officials as excepted under Section 82045(c).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal staff opinion issued in letter form rather than a formal opinion. The incoming request letter and company background materials are included with the FPPC response. No formal opinion was issued because the request did not raise a novel interpretation question.

QUESTION: May we continue to give these turkeys? If so, what reporting requirements need we and the recipients follow?

CONCLUSION: It is the informal view of the staff that your gifts of smoked turkeys need not be reported by you so long as you engage in no other activities which constitute attempts to influence state legislative or administrative action. On the basis of these facts, you may continue to make gifts of smoked turkeys to these officials without incurring any reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC Response No. 75-210 to Nicholas Turkey Breeding Farms regarding whether gifts of smoked turkeys to state legislators and agency officials require reporting. Staff concluded no reporting is required as the gifts fall within the exemption for payments that directly benefit state officials.
